---
title: "Does Risk-Based Inspection Fill the Gaps the Mandatory System Left Behind?"
subtitle: "FDNY RBIS, Mandatory Inspections, and Structural Fires by Council District — First Draft"
author: "NYC Council Data Team"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    css: ../assets/style.css
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 2
    fig_width: 9
    fig_height: 5.5
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
                      cache = FALSE)
# Rmd lives in code/ but all pipeline paths are relative to the project root.
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath(".."))
```

```{r pipeline, include=FALSE}
# Runs the full pipeline: dependencies -> data prep -> rates/plots -> models.
# Heavy on first run (open-data downloads + councilcount virtualenv).
# root.dir (set in setup) applies from this chunk onward.
source("code/04_analysis.R")
# Maps are included as static PNGs from visuals/ (generated by 03_mapping.R's
# mapshot step) to keep the report file small; no leaflet build needed here.

cor_dose_gap  <- dose_cd[!is.na(gap_dose), cor(rbis_dose, gap_dose)]
cor_dose_fire <- dose_cd[!is.na(pre_fidd_rate), cor(rbis_dose, pre_fidd_rate)]
cor_resid_gap <- dose_cd[!is.na(rbis_resid) & !is.na(gap_dose), cor(rbis_resid, gap_dose)]
rho_gap_rank  <- cd_gap[, cor(gap_dose, gap_rank, method = "spearman")]
n_cd          <- dose_cd[, uniqueN(citycouncildistrict)]

# p-values for the two gap-relationship scatters (for on-plot annotation)
p_resid_gap <- dose_cd[!is.na(rbis_resid) & !is.na(gap_dose), cor.test(rbis_resid, gap_dose)$p.value]
p_dose_gap  <- dose_cd[!is.na(gap_dose), cor.test(rbis_dose, gap_dose)$p.value]
fmt_p       <- function(p) if (p < 0.001) "p < 0.001" else paste0("p = ", round(p, 3))
max_undercov  <- cd_gap[, 1 - exp(-max(gap_dose))]  # largest pre-2014 under-coverage vs fire

# Denominator robustness: the per-building fire/inspection association net of
# building stock (partial correlation of log-counts given log-buildings).
.fire_ct <- fidd_by_cd_year[year < rbis_year, .(fire_count = sum(fidd_n)), by = citycouncildistrict]
.rbis_ct <- rbis_by_cd_year[year >= rbis_year, .(rbis_count = sum(rbis_n)), by = citycouncildistrict]
.cd_bldg <- bldgs_by_cd[year < boundary_change_year, .(bldg_avg = mean(total_bldgs)), by = citycouncildistrict]
.dd <- Reduce(function(a, b) merge(a, b, by = "citycouncildistrict"),
              list(.fire_ct, .rbis_ct, .cd_bldg))
partial_fire_rbis <- cor(resid(lm(log(fire_count) ~ log(bldg_avg), .dd)),
                         resid(lm(log(rbis_count) ~ log(bldg_avg), .dd)))

# Fire -> inspection standouts (is any district under-inspected vs its fire?)
n_under_rbis  <- fire_standouts[standout == "below predicted", .N]
over_rbis_fdr <- fire_standouts[standout == "above predicted"][order(-stud_resid)]
cd1_over      <- fire_standouts[citycouncildistrict == 1]

# Group test: high-fire bloc elevation above the RBIS-vs-gap line
.grp_coef    <- summary(gap_group_fit)$coefficients["fire_groupHigh-fire", ]
grp_b        <- .grp_coef["Estimate"]
grp_p        <- .grp_coef["Pr(>|t|)"]
grp_b_robust <- summary(gap_group_fit_robust)$coefficients["fire_groupHigh-fire", "Estimate"]

# Distributed-lag (model B) lagged coefficients + significance
.dl_ct <- summary(dl_mod)$coeftable
dl_b1 <- .dl_ct["l(insp_rate, 1)", "Estimate"]
dl_b2 <- .dl_ct["l(insp_rate, 2)", "Estimate"]
dl_b3 <- .dl_ct["l(insp_rate, 3)", "Estimate"]
dl_p3 <- .dl_ct["l(insp_rate, 3)", "Pr(>|t|)"]

# MIFC allocation stability: is the 2013 gap baseline representative of later
# MIFC years? Minimum Spearman of 2013 district rates vs each later year.
.mr     <- mifc_stab$ranks
.base13 <- .mr[year == 2013, .(citycouncildistrict, r13 = rate)]
.lateryrs <- setdiff(sort(unique(.mr$year)), 2013)
mifc_rep_min <- min(vapply(.lateryrs, function(y) {
  m <- merge(.base13, .mr[year == y, .(citycouncildistrict, ry = rate)], by = "citycouncildistrict")
  cor(m$r13, m$ry, method = "spearman")
}, numeric(1)))

# Fire-geography stability (validates the time-invariant high-fire grouping)
fire_consec_rng <- consec_all[regime == "Fires (FIDD)", range(spearman)]
fire_first_last <- ranks_all[regime == "Fires (FIDD)"][, {
  yrs <- sort(unique(year))
  m <- merge(.SD[year == min(yrs), .(citycouncildistrict, r1 = rank)],
             .SD[year == max(yrs), .(citycouncildistrict, r2 = rank)],
             by = "citycouncildistrict")
  .(rho = cor(m$r1, m$r2, method = "spearman"), y1 = min(yrs), y2 = max(yrs))
}]
```

## Executive Summary

In 2014 the FDNY replaced scheduled inspection cycles with the Risk-Based
Inspection System (RBIS), which uses a risk model to direct building
inspections toward the structures it scores as most fire-prone. This analysis
attempts to answer a question: **does RBIS direct inspection resources toward the
council districts that the mandatory inspection system (MIFC) under-served
relative to their fire levels?**

Three findings, in order of confidence:

1. **RBIS tracks fire levels overall.** Districts with higher pre-2014
   structural fire rates receive more RBIS inspections per building
   (r = `r round(cor_dose_fire, 2)` across `r n_cd` districts), and no district
   is significantly under-inspected relative to its fire level. The only
   significant deviations run the other way — over-inspection.

2. **But net of fire levels, RBIS partly carries over the mandatory system's
   coverage imbalances rather than correcting them.** Districts that received
   less mandatory coverage than their fire levels predict tend to receive less
   RBIS coverage too — a moderate but robust association
   (r = `r round(cor_resid_gap, 2)`, p < 0.001), not a one-to-one match.
   Citywide, RBIS allocation is unrelated to the prior coverage gap itself
   (r = `r round(cor_dose_gap, 2)`, not significant).

3. **High-fire districts as a group are over-covered relative to the gap.**
   Although RBIS allocation is unrelated to the prior coverage gap citywide,
   the high-fire districts (top tercile of pre-2014 fire rate) collectively
   receive RBIS coverage significantly above what their gap predicts
   (+`r round(grp_b, 3)` inspections per building, p < 0.001; robust to dropping
   the single largest district, +`r round(grp_b_robust, 3)`). RBIS allocates by
   fire level over and above the legacy mandatory-inspection pattern. (A
   district-by-district outlier test flags only one district on its own, because
   a coherent bloc above the line masks its members individually; the group test
   recovers the bloc.)

Evidence that RBIS *reduced* fires in the districts it targeted is **not
established** by this analysis. High-fire districts were already on a long
secular fire decline before 2014, violating the parallel-trends assumption the
event study requires; the apparent post-2014 effect disappears once each
district is given its own time trend, and a placebo test reproduces the
"effect" in a period with no treatment. The finding is a limit of the
observational design, not evidence that RBIS is ineffective.

## Background

- **MIFC** — Mandatory Inspections by Fire Companies: inspections required by
  building/occupancy category on fixed cycles, regardless of measured risk.
- **RBIS** — Risk-Based Inspection System (introduced 2014): an algorithmic
  risk model ranks buildings and fire companies inspect the highest-scoring
  structures first.
- **FIDD** — Fire Incident Dispatch Data: structural fire incidents, used here
  as the measure of realized fire risk.

All measures are aggregated to council district (CD) × year and normalized to
rates per building using annual MapPLUTO building counts.

RBIS begins in 2014 and ramps up over the following years; the mandatory program
continues alongside it. The analysis below treats the pre-2014 period as the
mandatory-only baseline and the post-2014 period as the risk-based era.

```{r insp-volume, fig.cap="Citywide inspection volume by regime. RBIS begins in 2014 and grows; the mandatory program continues."}
vol_long <- melt(dose_by_year, id.vars = "year",
                 measure.vars = c("mifc", "rbis", "total"),
                 variable.name = "source", value.name = "n")
ggplot(vol_long, aes(x = year, y = n, color = source)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = rbis_year - 0.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey40") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_width(2)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("mifc" = "grey50", "rbis" = "firebrick", "total" = "black"),
                     labels = c("MIFC", "RBIS", "Total")) +
  labs(title = "Citywide Inspection Volume by Regime",
       subtitle = "RBIS introduced 2014", x = NULL, y = "Inspections", color = NULL) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

## Data and Methods

| Source | NYC Open Data ID | Role |
|---|---|---|
| RBIS inspections | `itd7-gx3g` | Treatment: risk-based inspection activity |
| Mandatory inspections (MIFC) | `kfgh-h6re` | Baseline coverage regime |
| Fire Incident Dispatch Data | `8m42-w767` | Outcome: structural fires |
| MapPLUTO (annual vintages) | — | Denominator: buildings per CD per year |
| CouncilCount (ACS) | — | District demographics |

Fires and inspections are assigned to council districts using each dataset's
own coded `citycouncildistrict` field, as published in NYC Open Data. (An
alarm-box-to-PLUTO geocoding routine exists for optional block-level analysis
in `01b_block-geocoding.R`, but the district-level analysis here does not use
it.)

Two constructed district-level measures drive the targeting analysis:

- **Gap dose** — how much *less* mandatory coverage a district received than
  its fire level predicts. Computed as the (sign-flipped) residual of a
  log-log regression of the MIFC rate on the fire rate across districts. The
  MIFC rate uses 2013 — the first and only pre-RBIS year in the
  mandatory-inspection dataset — and the fire rate pools 2005–2013. The 2013
  baseline is representative, not idiosyncratic: MIFC's cross-district allocation
  is structurally stable, and 2013 correlates with every later MIFC year at
  Spearman ρ ≥ `r round(mifc_rep_min, 2)`. The log-log form is required because
  coverage scales super-linearly with fire rate in levels. The measure is robust
  to functional form: a rank-based version (fire rank minus coverage rank) agrees
  with Spearman ρ = `r round(rho_gap_rank, 2)`.
- **RBIS-exposure dose** — each district's mean post-2014 RBIS inspections per
  building: what RBIS actually did, rather than what any risk score said.

Both measures collapse multiple years into one number per district, which is
only valid if district fire risk is stable over time. It is: ranking districts
by structural fire rate is almost perfectly stable year to year
(Spearman `r round(fire_consec_rng[1], 2)`–`r round(fire_consec_rng[2], 2)`),
and the `r fire_first_last$y1`-vs-`r fire_first_last$y2` ranking — `r fire_first_last$y2 - fire_first_last$y1`
years apart — still correlates ρ = `r round(fire_first_last$rho, 2)`. The
districts that burn most do not change. This persistence is also what makes
risk-based targeting coherent in principle: the high-need districts are
identifiable years in advance, so a persistently under-served high-fire
district cannot be explained away by shifting fire risk.

## Fire Risk and Demographics

```{r fire-trends, fig.cap="Structural fire rate by council district over time, high-fire districts (top tercile of pre-2014 rate) highlighted."}
fire_trends <- merge(fidd_by_cd_year,
                     cd_fire_need[, .(citycouncildistrict, fire_group)],
                     by = "citycouncildistrict", all.x = TRUE)
fire_trends[is.na(fire_group), fire_group := "Lower-fire"]

ggplot(fire_trends, aes(x = date, y = fidd_rate, group = citycouncildistrict,
                        color = fire_group)) +
  geom_line(data = fire_trends[fire_group == "Lower-fire"], linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(data = fire_trends[fire_group == "High-fire"], linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date(rbis_start), linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = as.Date(rbis_start), y = Inf, label = "RBIS introduced",
           hjust = -0.05, vjust = 1.5, size = 3) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%Y", date_breaks = "2 years") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("High-fire" = "firebrick", "Lower-fire" = "grey75")) +
  labs(title = "Structural Fire Rate by Council District",
       subtitle = "High-fire districts (top tercile of pre-2014 fire rate) in red",
       x = NULL, y = "Fires per Building", color = NULL) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

Structural fire rates vary several-fold across districts and decline citywide
over the study period. The high-fire districts highlighted above — the top
tercile of pre-2014 fire rates — are the group the rest of the analysis follows,
and they differ demographically from the rest of the city. (District
demographics use 2013-boundary ACS estimates, matching the pre-2023 boundaries
the fire and inspection data sit on.)

```{r demo-highfire, fig.cap="Demographic composition: high-fire districts vs. city average."}
ggplot(demo_groups_stacked, aes(x = percent, y = group, fill = demographic)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(data = demo_groups_stacked[percent >= 5],
            aes(label = paste0(round(percent, 1), "%")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3,
            color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_nycc() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r demo-cor, fig.height=4, fig.cap="Correlation between district demographic shares and structural fire rates."}
ggplot(demo_fire_cor, aes(x = r, y = reorder(demographic, r), fill = r > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "firebrick", "FALSE" = "steelblue"),
                    guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Pearson r with fire rate", y = NULL) +
  theme_nycc()
```

```{r demo-stats, include=FALSE}
asian_r  <- demo_fire_cor[demographic == "Asian alone", r]
multi_r  <- demo_fire_cor[demographic == "Two or more races", r]
other_r  <- demo_fire_cor[demographic == "Some other race alone", r]
white_r  <- demo_fire_cor[demographic == "White alone", r]
demo_g   <- dcast(demo_by_group, demographic ~ fire_group, value.var = "percent")
asian_hi <- demo_g[demographic == "Asian alone", `High-fire`]
asian_lo <- demo_g[demographic == "Asian alone", `Lower-fire`]
```

The pattern is not a simple white / non-white split — the White share is
essentially flat across high- and lower-fire districts
(r = `r round(white_r, 2)`). The clearest signals are an **under-representation
of Asian residents** in high-fire districts (`r round(asian_hi, 1)`% vs.
`r round(asian_lo, 1)`% in lower-fire districts; r = `r round(asian_r, 2)`) and
an over-representation of residents reporting "two or more races"
(r = `r round(multi_r, 2)`) or "some other race" (r = `r round(other_r, 2)`).

This is a cross-sectional association, not a causal claim: demographics proxy
for housing stock, density, and income, which are the plausible drivers of
fire risk. It establishes that inspection allocation is an equity question.

> **Note on race categories.** "Some other race" and "two or more races" are
> the ACS race categories where much of the Hispanic/Latino population is
> recorded (Hispanic origin is collected as a separate ethnicity question), so
> their positive correlations with fire rate likely partly reflect Hispanic
> share. A direct Hispanic-origin measure would be needed to disambiguate this.

## The Coverage Gap (Setup)

```{r gap-scatter, fig.cap="Pre-2014 mandatory inspection coverage vs. fire rate, one point per district (log-log)."}
ggplot(cd_gap, aes(x = pre_fidd_rate, y = pre_mifc_rate)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(color = "firebrick", size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = citycouncildistrict), vjust = -0.8, size = 2.8) +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "Structural fires per building (log)",
       y = "MIFC inspections per building (log)") +
  theme_nycc()
```

Mandatory coverage rises with fire rate on average — the mandatory system was
not blind to risk. But districts scatter around the line, and the residual is
the policy-relevant quantity: districts **below** the line received less
mandatory coverage than districts with comparable fire levels. In the most
under-served cases the shortfall reaches roughly `r round(max_undercov * 100)`%
less coverage than the district's fire level predicts.

## Finding: How RBIS Allocates Inspections (Tracks Fires, Mirrors the Old Gaps)

Districts with higher pre-2014 fire rates get more RBIS inspections per building
(r = `r round(cor_dose_fire, 2)`): the risk model does send inspectors toward
fire-prone districts. Much of this link could just reflect building
stock: districts with fewer, older buildings have both more fires and more
inspections per building. Net of building stock the association is weaker but
still positive (partial r = `r round(partial_fire_rbis, 2)`).

The same holds when each district is tested individually. Regressing RBIS
coverage on fire rate and flagging districts that depart from the line
(studentized residuals, false-discovery-rate corrected across all `r n_cd`
districts), **`r n_under_rbis` districts are significantly under-inspected
relative to their fire level** — no high-fire district is being left behind. The only significant departures
run the other way — over-inspection. The most pronounced is a single district
dominated by commercial and high-rise occupancy, which receives roughly
`r round(cd1_over$rbis_dose / cd1_over$predicted, 1)`× the RBIS coverage its fire
rate predicts (`r round(cd1_over$rbis_dose, 3)` vs.
`r round(cd1_over$predicted, 3)` inspections per building) — likely reflecting
the risk model's weighting of commercial and high-rise occupancy rather than
residential fire incidence (see table).

```{r rbis-fire-scatter, fig.cap="RBIS coverage vs. pre-2014 fire rate. RBIS coverage rises with fire; the only significant outlier is over-inspection (red)."}
ggplot(fire_standouts, aes(x = pre_fidd_rate, y = rbis_dose)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(color = standout), size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = citycouncildistrict, color = standout),
            vjust = -0.8, size = 2.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("on trend" = "grey55",
                                "above predicted" = "firebrick",
                                "below predicted" = "steelblue")) +
  labs(x = "Structural fires per building (pre-2014)",
       y = "RBIS inspections per building (post-2014 mean)", color = NULL) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

```{r fire-standout-table}
over_tbl <- over_rbis_fdr[, .(`Council District` = citycouncildistrict,
                              `Fires per building (pre-2014)` = round(pre_fidd_rate, 3),
                              `RBIS per building` = round(rbis_dose, 3),
                              `Predicted from fire rate` = round(predicted, 3),
                              `p (FDR)` = round(p_fdr, 3))]
knitr::kable(over_tbl,
             caption = "Districts significantly over-inspected relative to their structural fire rate (FDR < 0.05). No district is significantly under-inspected.")
```

So on the fire-to-inspection dimension RBIS broadly does its job. The
gap-filling hypothesis, however, requires more — that RBIS
*preferentially* covers the districts the mandatory system left behind. Two
comparisons answer two different questions: whether RBIS *fills* the gap, and
what its allocation looks like *relative to* the gap. RBIS neither fills the gap
nor is neutral to it — on average, it does not send more inspections to the under-served
districts, and net of fire it partly mirrors the old coverage pattern.

- *Does RBIS fill the gap?* RBIS exposure vs. prior coverage gap:
  r = `r round(cor_dose_gap, 2)` (n.s.) — no systematic relationship. RBIS does
  not preferentially cover the under-served districts.
- *What is the pattern instead?* A residual-to-residual comparison purges fire
  levels from both sides — each
  regime's coverage is regressed on fire rate first, and the leftovers are
  correlated. The correlation between RBIS coverage net of fire and the prior
  gap is **r = `r round(cor_resid_gap, 2)` (p < 0.001)** — negative. Districts the
  mandatory system over-covered relative to their fires *tend to be* the ones
  RBIS over-covers too. This is a moderate but robust association, not a
  lockstep: it accounts for about `r round(cor_resid_gap^2 * 100)`% of the
  variation, leaving most of it district-specific. The two regimes partly share
  an allocation pattern; plausibly both are driven in part by the same
  building-stock and occupancy characteristics rather than by realized fire
  incidence.

```{r resid-scatter, fig.cap="RBIS coverage net of fire vs. the prior coverage gap (both fire-adjusted). The downward slope is the residual-to-residual association."}
resid_scatter <- merge(dose_cd[!is.na(rbis_resid) & !is.na(gap_dose)],
                       cd_fire_need[, .(citycouncildistrict, fire_group)],
                       by = "citycouncildistrict", all.x = TRUE)
resid_scatter[is.na(fire_group), fire_group := "Lower-fire"]

ggplot(resid_scatter, aes(x = gap_dose, y = rbis_resid)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey60") +
  geom_point(aes(color = fire_group), size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = citycouncildistrict, color = fire_group),
            vjust = -0.8, size = 2.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("High-fire" = "firebrick", "Lower-fire" = "grey65")) +
  labs(x = "Gap dose (MIFC under-coverage, net of fire)",
       y = "RBIS coverage net of fire (residual)", color = NULL,
       subtitle = sprintf("r = %.2f, %s  (r² = %.2f — a moderate association, most variation district-specific)",
                          cor_resid_gap, fmt_p(p_resid_gap), cor_resid_gap^2)) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

The slope of that scatter is the −`r abs(round(cor_resid_gap, 2))` correlation:
districts the mandatory system under-served (right) tend to get less RBIS than
their fire level predicts (lower), and vice versa.

```{r dose-scatter, fig.cap="RBIS exposure vs. prior under-inspection gap, high-fire districts highlighted. The gap-filling hypothesis predicts an upward slope."}
dose_scatter <- merge(dose_cd[!is.na(gap_dose)],
                      cd_fire_need[, .(citycouncildistrict, fire_group)],
                      by = "citycouncildistrict", all.x = TRUE)
dose_scatter[is.na(fire_group), fire_group := "Lower-fire"]

ggplot(dose_scatter, aes(x = gap_dose, y = rbis_dose)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(color = fire_group), size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = citycouncildistrict, color = fire_group),
            vjust = -0.8, size = 2.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("High-fire" = "firebrick", "Lower-fire" = "grey65")) +
  labs(x = "Gap dose (under-inspection under mandatory system)",
       y = "RBIS inspections per building (post-2014 mean)",
       color = NULL,
       subtitle = sprintf("r = %.2f, %s  (RBIS exposure is unrelated to the prior gap)",
                          cor_dose_gap, fmt_p(p_dose_gap))) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

A cluster of points sits visibly above the weakly-negative line. A
district-by-district outlier test flags only one of them, because a coherent
group above the line pulls the fitted line toward itself and deflates each
member's residual (masking). Testing the bloc directly — adding a high-fire
indicator (defined on fire rate, exogenous to RBIS) to the regression — confirms
the pattern: high-fire districts as a group receive **+`r round(grp_b, 3)`
inspections per building above what their gap predicts (p < 0.001)**, robust to
dropping the single largest district (+`r round(grp_b_robust, 3)`). The reading
is consistent with the rest of this section: RBIS does not fill the mandatory
system's relative gaps, but it does allocate by fire level, which lifts the
high-fire bloc above the gap line.

These two results are not in tension. They sit on different axes. *Between*
fire levels, RBIS follows fire — high-fire districts get more, which is why the
bloc floats above the line. *Within* a fire level, RBIS reproduces the mandatory
system's relative shortfalls — the districts MIFC under-served stay
under-served. In the same regression both appear together: the fire-group term
is positive (+`r round(grp_b, 3)`) while the gap term is negative. Concretely, a
high-fire district that the mandatory system also under-served still receives
more RBIS than a low-fire district (fire wins) but less than a high-fire
district the mandatory system covered fully (the gap persists). The high-fire
bloc sits high because of its fire level, not because RBIS corrected the gap.

```{r underserved-table}
underserved <- dose_cd[!is.na(gap_dose)][order(-gap_dose)][1:10,
  .(`Council District` = citycouncildistrict,
    `Gap dose (higher = more under-served pre-2014)` = round(gap_dose, 2),
    `RBIS inspections per building` = round(rbis_dose, 3))]
knitr::kable(underserved,
             caption = "Ten most under-served districts under the mandatory system, with their RBIS coverage.")
```

### Geographic view

The same allocation seen on the map: each district shaded by inspections per
building, with structural-fire rates as overlaid icons (larger = more fires).
The mandatory baseline (2013) and the risk-based era (2022) are shown
separately. (Static snapshots; the interactive version is `03_mapping.R`.)

```{r map-mifc, fig.cap="MIFC inspections per building by council district (2013), with structural-fire rates as icons.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("visuals/inspections_map_2013mifc_2013fidd.png")
```

```{r map-rbis, fig.cap="RBIS inspections per building by council district (2022), with structural-fire rates as icons.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("visuals/inspections_map_2022rbis_2013fidd.png")
```

## Finding: Did Fires Fall Where RBIS Went? (No Evidence Either Way)

We fit an event study interacting each district's RBIS-exposure dose with year
indicators, and a two-way fixed-effects model of fire rates on inspection rates
at lags 0–3 with district-clustered standard errors (a Wooldridge test confirms
serial correlation in the panel residuals). The event study is:

$$\text{fireRate}_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \sum_{k \neq 2013} \beta_k\,\big(\text{dose}_i \times \mathbb{1}[t=k]\big) + \varepsilon_{i,t}$$

for district $i$, year $t$. Here $\alpha_i$ is a district fixed effect (each
district compared to itself), $\gamma_t$ a year fixed effect (citywide shocks),
$\text{dose}_i$ the district's time-invariant RBIS-exposure dose, and $\beta_k$
the coefficient for year $k$ — the gap in fire rate per unit of dose, relative
to the reference year 2013 (so $\beta_{2013}=0$). The plotted $\beta_k$ should be
flat before 2014 and dip below zero after, if RBIS reduced fires. Standard errors
are clustered by district.

The naive event study *appears* to show fires falling in high-dose districts
after 2014. That reading does not survive scrutiny, for three reasons.

```{r event-study, fig.cap="Event study: fire rate by RBIS-exposure dose. Left: district + year fixed effects. Pre-2014 coefficients are not flat.", fig.height=4.5}
iplot(es_rbis, main = "",
      xlab = "Year", ylab = "Dose x year coefficient (per SD of dose)")
abline(v = rbis_year - 0.5, lty = 3, col = "grey40")
```

**1. Pre-trends fail.** The pre-2014 coefficients are not flat — high-dose
districts were already converging toward the rest of the city, a continuation
of the city's long structural fire decline. Parallel trends, which the causal
reading requires, does not hold.

**2. The effect vanishes under district-specific trends.** We give each district
its own linear time trend, replacing the district intercept with an intercept
*plus* a district-specific slope:

$$\alpha_i \;\longrightarrow\; \alpha_i + \delta_i\, t$$

so $\delta_i$ is a separate fire-rate trend for each district. The $\beta_k$ now
measure fire rates *relative to each district's own trajectory*, and a real
effect would show as a bend away from that trend at 2014. Instead the
coefficients scatter around zero — fires landed where each district's
pre-existing decline predicted. The apparent post-2014 drop was the trend, not
RBIS.

```{r event-study-trend, fig.cap="Event study with district-specific linear trends. The post-2014 effect disappears.", fig.height=4.5}
iplot(es_rbis_trend, main = "",
      xlab = "Year", ylab = "Dose x year coefficient, net of each CD's trend")
abline(v = rbis_year - 0.5, lty = 3, col = "grey40")
```

**3. A placebo finds a spurious effect.** Pretending RBIS launched in 2010 and
using only pre-2014 data — when nothing happened — the same design still
"detects" a significant fire decline in high-dose districts. A method that finds
an effect where none can exist cannot be trusted to measure the real one.

**Conclusion:** the event study **cannot establish that RBIS reduced fires**, in
either direction. The post-2014 pattern is an artifact of the secular fire
decline in the districts RBIS targets. This is a limitation of the observational
design, not evidence that RBIS is ineffective.

The distributed-lag model is the more credible causal-adjacent test. It uses
*annual* inspection rates (not a static dose) and lags them:

$$\text{fireRate}_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \sum_{L=0}^{3} \beta_L\,\text{inspRate}_{i,\,t-L} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$$

where $\text{inspRate}_{i,\,t-L}$ is the combined MIFC + RBIS inspection rate in
district $i$, $L$ years before year $t$, and $\beta_L$ is its effect on the
current fire rate (district and year fixed effects, district-clustered standard
errors). The same-year term $\beta_0$ is contaminated by simultaneity —
inspectors are sent *because* fires are occurring — so the causally meaningful
terms are the lags $\beta_1,\beta_2,\beta_3$: past inspections precede current
fires, so a negative lagged coefficient would mean inspections reduce *future*
fires.

```{r dist-lag, fig.cap="Distributed-lag estimates: inspection rate vs. fire rate, lags 0-3, district and year fixed effects."}
coefplot(dl_mod, main = "", xlab = "Inspection rate lag (years)")
abline(h = 0, lty = 3, col = "grey40")
```

Its estimates are mixed and mostly weak. The lagged terms — the ones that break
same-year simultaneity — show no significant association at one or two years
(b = `r round(dl_b1, 3)` and `r round(dl_b2, 3)`, both n.s.); only the three-year
lag is negative and significant (b = `r round(dl_b3, 3)`, p < 0.001, and stable
when a building-stock control is added). An effect that appears at three years
but not at one or two is inconsistent, so this is suggestive at most — not
strong evidence that inspections reduce fires. (Full estimates, including a
Poisson specification with a log-buildings offset, are in `04_analysis.R`.)

The allocation findings above stand independently of this section: they concern
how inspections are *allocated*, not whether they *reduce* fires.

## Caveats

1. **No random assignment.** RBIS targets risk by design; every estimate here
   is observational.
2. **Parallel trends fail** in the event studies (the fire-reduction analysis).
   Causal language about fire reduction is not warranted: the apparent effect
   disappears under district-specific trends and a placebo test reproduces it
   where no treatment occurred. The allocation findings are unaffected.
3. **District assignment is taken as given.** Records are placed in council
   districts using each dataset's published `citycouncildistrict` field; we rely
   on the publisher's geocoding and do not independently verify it. Districts
   were redrawn for 2023, so the analysis is restricted to pre-2023 years to keep
   each district a stable unit.
4. **Shared-regressor caveat.** The residual-vs-residual correlation in the
   allocation analysis conditions both measures on the same fire-rate variable;
   measurement error in fire rates would push the correlation negative
   mechanically. The fire measure is a nine-year pooled mean, and the rank-based
   gap measure agrees (ρ = `r round(rho_gap_rank, 2)`), so this is unlikely to
   account for the full effect.
5. **MIFC vs. RBIS overlap.** The two inspection datasets share some records
   and the boundary between "mandatory" and "risk-based" activity is not
   perfectly clean in the source data (see notes in `01_data-preparation.R`).
6. **Single-year mandatory baseline.** The MIFC dataset begins in 2013, so the
   gap dose rests on one pre-RBIS year of mandatory inspections (≈40,000
   records). This is a snapshot rather than a multi-year average, but a
   representative one: MIFC's district allocation is structurally stable, and
   2013 correlates with every later MIFC year at Spearman ρ ≥
   `r round(mifc_rep_min, 2)`, so it is unlikely to be an idiosyncratic year.
7. **Demographics are 2023 ACS estimates** applied to all years; district
   demographic change over the period is not modeled.

## Next Steps

- Save final figures to `visuals/` and add map panels from `03_mapping.R`
  (gap dose and RBIS dose choropleths).
- Confirm 2013 reference-year anomaly in the fire series before finalizing
  event-study figures.
- Quantify the overlap between the MIFC and RBIS source records, which
  underpins the mandatory-versus-risk-based framing.

## Addendum: High-Fire Defined as Top Quartile

The main analysis defines "high-fire" as the **top tercile** (top third) of
pre-2014 fire rate. As a sensitivity check, this addendum redefines it as the
**top quartile** (top 25%) and re-runs the outputs that depend on that cut.
Results that do not use the grouping — the demographic-vs-fire-rate correlation,
the coverage gap, RBIS-vs-fire, and the event study — are unchanged, because they
use the continuous fire rate, not the high/low label.

```{r q4-setup, include=FALSE}
cd_fire_q4 <- fidd_by_cd_year[year < rbis_year & !is.na(fidd_rate),
                              .(mean_fidd_rate = mean(fidd_rate)), by = citycouncildistrict][order(-mean_fidd_rate)]
cut_q4 <- quantile(cd_fire_q4$mean_fidd_rate, 3/4, na.rm = TRUE)
cd_fire_q4[, fire_group := ifelse(mean_fidd_rate >= cut_q4, "High-fire", "Lower-fire")]
n_q4 <- cd_fire_q4[fire_group == "High-fire", .N]
n_t3 <- cd_fire_need[fire_group == "High-fire", .N]

# Demographics under the quartile cut
demo_fire_q4 <- merge(as.data.table(demo_long),
                      cd_fire_q4[, .(citycouncildistrict, fire_group)], by = "citycouncildistrict")
demo_by_group_q4 <- demo_fire_q4[, .(percent = mean(percent, na.rm = TRUE)), by = .(fire_group, demographic)]
demo_ord_q4 <- demo_by_group_q4[, .(m = mean(percent)), by = demographic][order(-m), demographic]
demo_stack_q4 <- rbind(
  demo_by_group_q4[, .(group = fire_group, demographic, percent)],
  as.data.table(city_avg)[, .(group = "City Average", demographic, percent)]
)[, `:=`(group = factor(group, levels = c("High-fire", "Lower-fire", "City Average")),
         demographic = factor(demographic, levels = demo_ord_q4))]
asian_hi_q4 <- dcast(demo_by_group_q4, demographic ~ fire_group, value.var = "percent")[demographic == "Asian alone", `High-fire`]

# Group test under the quartile cut
grp_q4 <- merge(dose_cd[!is.na(gap_dose)], cd_fire_q4[, .(citycouncildistrict, fire_group)], by = "citycouncildistrict")
grp_q4[, fire_group := relevel(factor(fire_group), ref = "Lower-fire")]
fit_q4 <- lm(rbis_dose ~ gap_dose + fire_group, data = grp_q4)
grp_b_q4 <- summary(fit_q4)$coefficients["fire_groupHigh-fire", "Estimate"]
grp_p_q4 <- summary(fit_q4)$coefficients["fire_groupHigh-fire", "Pr(>|t|)"]

# Dose scatter highlighted by the quartile grouping
dose_scatter_q4 <- merge(dose_cd[!is.na(gap_dose)],
                         cd_fire_q4[, .(citycouncildistrict, fire_group)], by = "citycouncildistrict")
```

The quartile cut labels **`r n_q4`** districts high-fire (versus **`r n_t3`**
under the tercile) — a narrower, more extreme group.

```{r q4-demo, fig.cap="Demographic composition of high-fire districts, top-quartile definition."}
ggplot(demo_stack_q4, aes(x = percent, y = group, fill = demographic)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(data = demo_stack_q4[percent >= 5], aes(label = paste0(round(percent, 1), "%")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3, color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_nycc() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  labs(title = "Demographics of High-Fire Districts (Top Quartile)", x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Narrowing to the top quartile keeps only the most fire-prone districts, so the
demographic contrast is more concentrated. Asian residents make up
`r round(asian_hi_q4, 1)`% of the high-fire group under the quartile cut, versus
`r round(asian_hi, 1)`% under the tercile.

```{r q4-dose-scatter, fig.cap="RBIS exposure vs. gap, high-fire districts by the top-quartile definition."}
ggplot(dose_scatter_q4, aes(x = gap_dose, y = rbis_dose)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(color = fire_group), size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = citycouncildistrict, color = fire_group),
            vjust = -0.8, size = 2.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("High-fire" = "firebrick", "Lower-fire" = "grey65")) +
  labs(x = "Gap dose (under-inspection under mandatory system)",
       y = "RBIS inspections per building (post-2014 mean)", color = NULL) +
  theme_nycc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

The group test still holds under the narrower definition: the high-fire bloc
sits **+`r round(grp_b_q4, 3)`** inspections per building above the RBIS-vs-gap
line (`r fmt_p(grp_p_q4)`), compared with **+`r round(grp_b, 3)`** under the
tercile. The substantive conclusion — RBIS allocates by fire level over and above
the legacy pattern — is not sensitive to where the high-fire threshold is drawn.
